VOrchestra is pre-release. Focused changes with clear validation are more useful than broad rewrites.
VOrchestra releases are built from Git tags through .github/workflows/release.yml.
0.1.x - Stabilization
VOrchestra is a local-first desktop control center for Python virtual environments. It helps developers discover, inspect, repair, secure, clean up, and operate environments across local workspaces.
Diagnostics and security checks are intentionally manual. They do not auto-run when opening the tab.
Disk cleanup helps identify where Python environments and caches consume space.
VOrchestra can create environments directly or from project/template sources.
VOrchestra turns environment state into clear repair actions.
VOrchestra supports multiple environment managers with different mutation levels.
VOrchestra is organized around local Python environment maintenance.
Package Studio is the environment dependency workspace.
The project board changes the app from venv-first to project-first.
Studio includes project-level tools next to package and diagnostics workflows.
These screenshots show the current 0.1.0 pre-release interface. They are included to make the open source project easier to evaluate before binary releases are available.
Workspaces are directories VOrchestra scans for Python environments.

VOrchestra is a local-first Tauri 2 desktop app. The frontend owns UI state and orchestration; the Rust backend owns filesystem access, subprocess execution, package introspection, SQLite persistence and long-running jobs.
VOrchestra uses backend jobs for long-running work so the desktop UI remains responsive.
Tauri commands are internal app API. Treat them as semi-public because frontend behavior depends on names and payloads.
database is locked
Development window
VOrchestra is currently distributed from source. You need Node.js, npm, Rust, and Python.
Development requirements
VOrchestra is pre-release. Focused changes with clear validation are more useful than broad rewrites.
git clone https://github.com/marquesantero/vorchestra.git cd vorchestra npm install npm run tauri dev

Branch from main. Keep PRs focused on one behavior, screen, command group or doc area. Include a short test plan. Add tests when changing parsers, package operations, background jobs or core state hooks. Include screenshots/GIFs for visible UI changes. Update CHANGELOG.md when behavior changes.

Frontend: React functional components and hooks. Strict TypeScript. Avoid any; narrow untyped Tauri payloads at the boundary. Use shared visual utilities instead of ad hoc styles. Do not introduce native alert, prompt, or confirm. Backend: Keep commands domain-oriented under src-tauri/src/commands/. Heavy work must not block the UI thread. Use background jobs for long-running or cancellable work. Do not pass user-controlled strings to a shell. Preserve Conda/Pixi read-only behavior unless mutation is intentionally designed and tested.
VOrchestra releases are built from Git tags through .github/workflows/release.yml.

Before tagging: main is green in CI. CHANGELOG.md has a dated release entry. Version is consistent in package.json, src-tauri/Cargo.toml and src-tauri/tauri.conf.json. README reflects install state and known limitations. Real app smoke test passed with npm run tauri dev. Dependabot PRs likely to regress the app are closed or deferred.
npm run check npm run build cd src-tauri && cargo fmt --all -- --check cd src-tauri && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings cd src-tauri && cargo test --all-targets
git tag v0.1.0 git push origin v0.1.0 The release workflow creates a draft prerelease.

Goal: make the source-built app reliable for early testers. Run final real-app smoke test with npm run tauri dev. Validate create environment, template creation, workspace scan, package catalog, tree, graph, diagnostics, security, repair, cleanup, Docker and VS Code flows. Fix blockers found during real-app smoke testing. Triage Dependabot PRs conservatively. Add screenshots/GIFs. Convert good-first-issue candidates into GitHub issues.

Goal: install VOrchestra without Node/Rust. Validate .github/workflows/release.yml on a tag. Publish GitHub Release artifacts. Verify Linux .AppImage and .deb. Verify macOS .dmg. Verify Windows installer artifact. Document platform-specific install dependencies.

Goal: reduce correctness and performance risks in large real workspaces. Performance budget for workspace scan and Studio open. More fixtures for package metadata and unusual project manifests. More event-driven progress reporting where polling remains as fallback. Broader tests for package mutation, rebuild, import/export and cleanup. Clear SQLite migration policy.
Poetry/PDM/Hatch workflows. Remote SSH workspace inventory. Plugin API for custom Studio panels. Homebrew tap, winget, Flathub and Snap. Full i18n. Strictly opt-in telemetry only if users request it.
VOrchestra is a local-first desktop control center for Python virtual environments. It helps developers discover, inspect, repair, secure, clean up, and operate environments across local workspaces.

Inventory many .venv folders without remembering where each project keeps them. Detect broken, stale, outdated, vulnerable, oversized, or incomplete environments. Repair common issues without guessing the right pip, uv, pipdeptree, or pip-audit command. Understand dependency trees, package sizes, upgrade risk, lock drift, and why a package is installed. Run project-level operations such as VS Code interpreter setup, Jupyter kernel registration, Docker files, .env editing, pre-commit setup, and quick scripts.
VOrchestra does not replace uv, pip, Conda, Pixi, VS Code, Docker, Jupyter, or your shell. It coordinates those tools locally and shows their state in one place.
Version 0.1.0 is a pre-release source-built version. Binary installers are planned for the next release milestone.

Quick start: build and run the app. Product overview: understand the main workflows. Architecture: frontend, backend, jobs, and commands. Troubleshooting: common failures and fixes. Roadmap: planned release direction.
Diagnostics and security checks are intentionally manual. They do not auto-run when opening the tab.
Check Python and package manager consistency. Detect missing tools. Detect outdated packages. Adapt commands for pip or uv depending on manager type. Run heavy checks as cancellable background jobs.
Run pip-audit when available. Show install actions when pip-audit is missing. Summarize vulnerabilities using PyPA advisories. Export CycloneDX SBOM.
Metadata hygiene checks include: License summary. Missing license queue. Deprecated or inactive package hints. Suspicious package names and typosquatting hints. Searchable/filterable review queue.
Disk cleanup helps identify where Python environments and caches consume space.
Cache summary. Allow-listed cache purge. Duplicate wheel groups. Large environments. Stale environments. Missing inventory entries. Orphan package candidates.
Cleanup should be explainable before destructive action. Prefer preview, explicit confirmation, and recoverable trash for environment deletion/rebuild flows.
VOrchestra can create environments directly or from project/template sources.

Empty environment using pip. Empty environment using uv. Built-in template based on real community workflows. Custom template captured from an existing environment. Project manifests such as requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, Pipfile, setup.py, and setup.cfg.
When an environment is created from a template, VOrchestra stores and displays the template name on the environment card. Environments without a template show a personalized/custom label.
Template creation and package installation run as background jobs. The UI should remain responsive and cancellation should be explicit where the operation can take time.
uv-created environments should keep uv as the manager type. VOrchestra uses a workspace-local UV_CACHE_DIR for uv operations to avoid global cache permission failures.
VOrchestra turns environment state into clear repair actions.

Missing environment path. Broken Python executable. Missing pip. Missing helper tools. Outdated packages. Vulnerabilities. Lockfile drift. Stale database entry. Abnormal package/cache size. Native manager read-only limitations.

Remove stale inventory entry. Install missing pip when supported. Install pipdeptree or pip-audit. Re-sync from pyproject.toml or requirements.txt. Rebuild from project sources. Set VS Code interpreter. Export support bundle.
Rebuild flows should show the rebuild source before destructive action and use recoverable workspace-local trash where possible.
VOrchestra supports multiple environment managers with different mutation levels.
pip environments support package cataloging, package mutation, dependency tree helpers, diagnostics, security scans, package hygiene and repair actions.

uv environments support fast creation and uv-native project workflows where available: uv sync uv lock uv add uv remove uv run uv tree VOrchestra must account for uv CLI version differences. Commands should be built from supported arguments, not assumed from a newer version.
Conda and Pixi are detected as read-only inventory. VOrchestra can inspect and guide, but it should not mutate native manager metadata unless a future workflow is explicitly designed and tested.
VOrchestra is organized around local Python environment maintenance.

Inventory Find environments across workspaces and keep a fast local cache. Health Score environments by broken state, stale records, missing tools, drift and security issues. Repair Guide common fixes instead of leaving users to infer shell commands. Cleanup Find large/stale environments, duplicate wheels, caches and missing inventory records. Security Run audit, metadata hygiene, suspicious name hints and SBOM export. Project operations Work from project roots: manifests, lockfiles, Docker, VS Code, Jupyter, .env and scripts.
Find environments across workspaces and keep a fast local cache.
Score environments by broken state, stale records, missing tools, drift and security issues.
Guide common fixes instead of leaving users to infer shell commands.
Find large/stale environments, duplicate wheels, caches and missing inventory records.
Run audit, metadata hygiene, suspicious name hints and SBOM export.
Work from project roots: manifests, lockfiles, Docker, VS Code, Jupyter, .env and scripts.
VOrchestra should not compete with package managers on generic package management. Its advantage is transversal visibility across the machine and guided maintenance.
Package Studio is the environment dependency workspace.

Search, sort and filter installed packages. Install, uninstall and update packages. Export requirements. Search PyPI and choose package versions. Install from PyPI, Test PyPI, Git, URL, local file, or local project. Preview compatibility conflicts. See package sizes separately from initial package cataloging. Explore dependency tree and graph views. Ask why a package is installed. Preview upgrades before applying them.

For pip environments, pipdeptree is required for the richer tree view. If missing, VOrchestra shows install instructions and action buttons for the selected environment. For uv environments, VOrchestra adapts commands to uv where possible.
The project board changes the app from venv-first to project-first.
Project root. Associated environments. Best environment health. Detected manifests and lockfiles. Next-best actions.
Scan project. Open Studio. Run diagnostics. Repair environment. Sync from project manifests. Use uv-native workflows such as uv sync, uv lock, uv add, uv remove, and uv run when supported.
Studio includes project-level tools next to package and diagnostics workflows.
Structured .env editor. Awareness of .env.example and .env.template. Secret-like value masking. Raw edit mode. Read-only pyvenv.cfg viewer.

Saved automation scripts. Quick runners for pytest, ruff, black and mypy. Open activated terminal. Generate VS Code settings. VS Code Interpreter Doctor. Register Jupyter kernel. Generate Docker files. Run Docker build/run in a terminal. Install pre-commit hooks.

These screenshots show the current 0.1.0 pre-release interface. They are included to make the open source project easier to evaluate before binary releases are available. Workspace inventory with discovered environments, workspace controls, search and environment cards. Studio package overview with health summary, environment brief, package count and library manifest header. Library manifest with installed package list, package actions, search, sorting and filtering controls. Automation tab with quick tool runners and saved script editor for environment-specific tasks. Configuration tab with structured .env editing and read-only pyvenv.cfg inspection. Diagnostics dashboard with manual checks, package manager status, consistency check and outdated packages. Security view with pip-audit guidance plus package metadata audit, license and risk signals. Diagnostic findings with package risk details, outdated status and actionable environment recommendations. Lock tab for freezing dependencies, restoring lock state and importing lock or requirements files. Repair overview with health score, VS Code Interpreter Doctor, rebuild source preview and next repair action. Repair Wizard with recommended action cards for broken, stale, missing-tool and drift scenarios. Deploy tab with VS Code config, Jupyter kernel, pre-commit hooks and Docker run integrations. Docker deployment workflow with generated manifest files and preview before saving or running.
Workspaces are directories VOrchestra scans for Python environments.

Add and remove workspace roots. Mark a default workspace. Scan recursively for virtual environments. Adopt existing environments discovered on disk. Keep SQLite inventory in sync. Remove stale entries when a folder no longer exists. Cancel long scans and prevent deleted paths from being re-adopted by an active scan.

Avoid adding / as a workspace unless you intentionally want a broad scan. Large roots can be slow and may discover system paths that should not be managed. Use narrower roots such as: ~/projects ~/work /dados/python
Set VORCHESTRA_SCAN_MAX_DEPTH to control recursion depth: VORCHESTRA_SCAN_MAX_DEPTH=12 npm run tauri dev

VOrchestra is a local-first Tauri 2 desktop app. The frontend owns UI state and orchestration; the Rust backend owns filesystem access, subprocess execution, package introspection, SQLite persistence and long-running jobs.

src/App.tsx wires global state, overlays and high-level routing. src/hooks/app/ contains domain hooks split by responsibility: useAppInitialization.ts: bootstraps workspaces, cache, Python discovery, templates and manager status. useWorkspaceOperations.ts: workspace scans, single-venv sync and workspace CRUD. useVenvLifecycle.ts: create/delete/template-save flows. useStudioLoader.ts: Studio-adjacent data loading after selecting an environment. useUiPreferences.ts: shortcuts, theme and zoom. src/components/Studio/ contains the environment Studio panels. src/services/backgroundJobs.ts observes long-running backend jobs.

src-tauri/src/lib.rs registers Tauri commands and database migrations. src-tauri/src/jobs.rs tracks background job state, progress, logs, result and cancellation. src-tauri/src/helpers.rs contains shared command execution, Python/venv helpers, parsers and safety utilities. src-tauri/src/commands/ is split by feature area.

venv.rs: scan, create, clone, diff and details. packages.rs: install, update, uninstall, tree, sizes, PyPI and package hygiene. diagnostics.rs: diagnostics and security audit jobs. lockfile.rs: freeze, restore and drift. cache.rs: cache summary, duplicate wheel detection and purge. project.rs: manifest autodetect. system.rs: Python/runtime manager discovery and automation commands. integrations*.rs: terminals, VS Code, Jupyter, pre-commit and Docker. bundle.rs: export/import venv bundles. files.rs: .env, pyvenv.cfg and generated project files.

Anything that can recurse over directories, call Python, call pip/uv, or install packages should be a background job. UI should not auto-run expensive diagnostics on tab entry. Commands that mutate environments should surface progress and cancellation when possible. The app is local-first: no telemetry, no account, and network only for explicit package/search/audit operations.
VOrchestra uses backend jobs for long-running work so the desktop UI remains responsive.

Frontend invokes a start_*_job command. Backend returns a jobId immediately. Frontend observes the job through waitForBackgroundJob(jobId, onUpdate). Backend updates snapshots with status, progress, message, logs, result and error. Frontend may call cancel_background_job for cancellable jobs.

Status Meaning running Job is active. cancelling Cancellation was requested. cancelled Job observed cancellation and stopped. success Job completed with a result. error Job failed with an error message.

Heavy jobs need visible progress or at least clear running state. Cancellable jobs need a visible cancel action. The UI must stay responsive while diagnostics, scans and package operations run. Polling can remain as a fallback, but Tauri events should be the primary path where available.
Tauri commands are internal app API. Treat them as semi-public because frontend behavior depends on names and payloads.
Long-running commands should use start_*_job and return a jobId. Read-only quick commands may return values directly. Elevated or tool-install commands should be explicit.

Workspace scans. Venv details, package reads and size scans. Package install/update/uninstall/export/tree/hygiene/conflict preview. Diagnostics and security audit. Lockfile generate/restore/drift. Clone/diff/import/export bundle. Cache summary and purge. Runtime install/listing. Studio integrations for VS Code, Jupyter, pre-commit and Docker.

Never execute user-provided shell text through a shell unless quoting is controlled and the source is trusted. Prefer Command args arrays over interpolated strings. Validate filesystem paths before destructive operations. Use spawn_blocking for blocking filesystem/subprocess work. Use cancellation-aware command execution when a child process can take time.

This usually means two write operations tried to update SQLite at the same time or a long scan kept writing while another mutation ran. Actions: Wait for active scans/jobs to finish or cancel them. Avoid scanning very broad roots like /. Retry after the current job has stopped. Report the sequence if it repeats; include workspace path, action attempted and app logs.

Possible causes: Environment Python is broken. pip is missing. Manager type was detected incorrectly. A previous scan/job is still active. Actions: Open Repair Wizard for the environment. Install missing pip or helper tools if prompted. Cancel active jobs and retry package load. Use a narrow workspace scan.
documents.s: Package catalog loops or never finishes

documents.t: For pip environments, install pipdeptree in the selected environment: python -m pip install pipdeptree For uv environments, VOrchestra adapts commands where supported. If uv tree fails, check your uv version: uv --version uv tree --help
documents.s: Dependency tree says helper is missing

documents.t: Install pip-audit in the selected environment: python -m pip install pip-audit If the environment has no pip, repair that first.

Install WebKit/GTK prerequisites for your distribution. On Debian/Ubuntu-like systems: sudo apt-get install -y \ libwebkit2gtk-4.1-dev \ libgtk-3-dev \ libayatana-appindicator3-dev \ librsvg2-dev \ libsoup-3.0-dev \ libjavascriptcoregtk-4.1-dev
Check that the environment manager is detected as uv, not pip, and that project manifests are valid. If the issue repeats, include the template name, manager, manifest files and logs.

The app can run offline for inventory, local package cataloging, local diagnostics, .env, VS Code settings, Docker file generation and many repair views. Network is needed for PyPI search, package installation, security advisory retrieval and dependency downloads.
npm install npm run tauri dev
npm run dev
npm run tauri build The release workflow is scaffolded, but binary releases have not been published yet.

Use targeted checks while developing: npm run check:frontend npm run test:frontend:product npm run check:rust npm run test:rust Run the full check before large pushes: npm run check Rust-specific checks: cd src-tauri cargo fmt --all -- --check cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings cargo test --all-targets
VOrchestra is currently distributed from source. You need Node.js, npm, Rust, and Python.
git clone https://github.com/marquesantero/vorchestra.git cd vorchestra npm install npm run tauri dev The app opens as a Tauri desktop window.

Add a workspace that contains Python projects. Scan the workspace. Select an environment or create a new one. Open Studio for that environment. Load packages, run diagnostics, review security, and inspect repair suggestions.

Install these if you want the full product surface: python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip install pipdeptree pip-audit curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh pipdeptree enables richer dependency tree views. pip-audit enables security scans. uv enables faster environment creation and project workflows.

Tool Minimum Purpose Node.js 20+ Frontend tooling and Docusaurus docs npm bundled with Node Dependency install and scripts Rust 1.85+ Tauri backend Python 3.x Environment creation and package inspection

Tool Required Purpose pip Recommended Default package manager and fallback operations uv Optional Fast creation, sync, lock, add/remove, uv-native project flows pipdeptree Optional Dependency tree and graph support for pip environments pip-audit Optional Security scan through PyPA advisories Docker Optional Docker file generation and build/run terminal action Git Optional pre-commit setup and project operations VS Code CLI Optional Interpreter doctor and settings integration Jupyter/ipykernel Optional Kernel registration Conda/Pixi Optional Read-only native manager inventory

On Debian/Ubuntu-like systems, Tauri usually needs packages similar to: sudo apt-get install -y \ libwebkit2gtk-4.1-dev \ libgtk-3-dev \ libayatana-appindicator3-dev \ librsvg2-dev \ libsoup-3.0-dev \ libjavascriptcoregtk-4.1-dev Check the Tauri prerequisites for your distribution if the build fails.
Variable Default Purpose VORCHESTRA_SCAN_MAX_DEPTH 16 Maximum workspace scan depth, clamped to 3..64.
